source_metadata.title_raw: Zach Friend - A-18-086 - May 31, 2018 - Santa Cruz

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
1 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Zach Friend 
Santa Cruz County Board of Supervisors 
701 Ocean Street Ste. 500 
Santa Cruz, C~ 95060 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-086 
Dear Mr. Friend: 
May 31, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the Act). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of°the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975} 
1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this 
is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from participating in decisions 
regarding the Santa Cruz Branch Rail Line (the "Rail Line") because your home is located 
approximately 250 feet from a Rail Line right of way? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Governmental decisions relating to the Rail Line will have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on your residential property. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-18-086 
Page No. 2 
You are a member of the Board of the Santa Cruz Regional Transportation Commission 
("RTC"). You are also the Second District Supervisor for Santa Cruz County, elected to that office 
in 2012 and reelected in 2016. The RTC's governing board is comprised of all five members of the 
Santa Cruz County Board of Supervisors, one member for each of the cities in the county, and three 
members appointed by the Santa Cruz Metropolitan Transit District. 
A. 
BacJ<,ground. 
The Santa Cruz Branch Rail Line is a 32-mile continuous rail corridor traversing the County 
of Santa Cruz. The corridor runs through highly urbanized portions of the County as well as more 
rural and agricultural areas. The RTC estimates that approximately 50% of the Santa Cruz County 
population currently lives within 1 mile of the rail line. 
The RTC purchased the rail corridor in 2012 using approximately $11 million in funding 
from the voter-approved Proposition 116, which requires that the rail line be used for "rail projects 
within Santa Cruz County which facilitate recreational, commuter, intercity and intercounty travel." 
As a condition of approval of funding for purchase of the line by the California Transportation 
Commission (CTC), the RTC was required to commit "to initiating passenger rail service" and "to 
be responsible for continuing freight rail service for so long as would be required by the Surface 
Transportation Board." The R TC board executed resolutions making these commitments, and 
entered an agreement with a rail operator and common carrier to provide these services and to 
acquire an easement for freight operations from the previous owner. Additionally, freight service 
for existing freight customers was part of the agreement as well as an agreement from the rail 
operator that a trail would be constructed alongside the rail line by local jurisdictions or the RTC. 
As part of the CTC requirements referenced above, the RTC is obligated to reimburse the 
State the amount of CTC funds used for the purchase of the line or the then-present market value of 
the line if it ceases to use the line for the original purpose approved by the CTC. 
Your home, valued over $2,000, is located within the Second Supervisorial District, 
approximately 250 feet from a Rail Line right of way. 
B. 
Potential Decisions. 
The RTC Board is expected to make various decisions regarding the Santa Cruz Branch Rail 
Line corridor, including. 
I. Selection of a Nelv Operator: The operator has notified the RTC of its intent to discontinue 
its operation of the line. The RTC issued an RFP and has engaged in contract negotiations 
for freight and passenger service with a proposed new operator. A vote on that contract is 
expected to take place at some point within the next month. Also, once a new operator is 
selected, the RTC Board is expected to make various decisions regarding rail operations by 
that operator, including decisions relating to tourist transportation services. 
· 


File No. A-18-086 
Page No. 3 
2. Implementation of a Planned Trail: The RTC has commenced implementation of the trail 
portion of the corridor use. The RTC approved a Master Plan for the trail, which spans the 
length of the rail corridor and is being implemented by partner agencies on a segment-by-
segment basis. None of the segments currently underway are within the Second 
Supervisorial District. 
3. Unified Corridor Study and Implementation of Recommendations for Future Use of the 
Corridor: Tfie RTC is engaged in a technical study regarding the highest best use of the rail 
corridor and other essential transit corridors in the County. The purpose of this study, known 
as the Unified Corridor Study ("UCS"), is to examine which transportation improvements 
work together to make the most effective use of the community's north/south transportation 
corridor, includihg three parallel routes, one of which is the Santa Cruz County Branch Rail 
Line. The UCS is expected to provide findings in late summer or early fall and may include 
recommendations to change tlie rail line's uses, such as removal of the tracks, or 
intensification of current uses, such as increased rail operations, in addition to 
recommendations regarding use of the other two parallel routes along Highway One and 
local frontage roads. The RTC Board is expected to take action to accept or approve the 
UCS, and act to implement recommendations of the UCS at some time after its completion. 
As the Second District Supervisor, you frequently hold informational "town hall" meetings 
and you will likely be requested to hold such meetings regarding the UCS and its recommendations. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a finan9ial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the 
official's interests identified in that section. Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a 
financial interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. 
Relevant to the Rail Line decisions, you have a real property interest in your residence. 
Accordingly, we must determine whether the financial effect on your real property resulting from 
the Rail Line decisions is foreseeable and material. 
A. 
Foreseeability and Materiality. 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether the financial interest is 
explicitly involved in the decision. Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the 
official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) 2 lfthe interest is "not explicitly involved" 
2 Regulation 1870 I (a) states that real property is the subject of a decision if the decision impacts the property 
as set forth in Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I )-(9). The Rail Line decisions do not involve any of these types of decisions for 
your residential property. 


File No. A-18-086 
Page No. 4 
in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely 
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) The Rail Line decisions do not 
explicitly involve your residence, so the standard for foreseeability is whether a financial effect is a 
"realistic possibility." 
To determine materiality regarding real property interests, Regulation 18702.2 states in 
relevant part that the financial effect of a decision is presumed material where the decision: 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property .... 
(Regulation l 8702.2(a).) 
You state that the RTC Board is expected to make various decisions regarding rail 
operations, including decisions relating to tourist transportation services that would be required of 
the new operator. Such decisions would necessarily entail a change in the intensity of use along the 
rail corridor, and would likely substantially alter, among other things, the noise, air quality, 
pedestrian and vehicle traffic, and air quality in your residential area, and involve changes to the 
character of your property that would affect the market value of your residence. Moreover, 
decisions regarding future use of the corridor based on the UCS would include the Rail Line right of 
way located about 250 feet from your home. Thus, you have a foreseeable material financial interest 
in the Rail Line decisions. 
The RTC's decisions regarding implementation of a planned trail are unclear based on the 
facts provided. You state that the plan is being implemented on a segment-by-segment basis, and 
none of the segments currently underway are within your supervisorial district. If decisions are 
necessary to implement the trail in accordance with the RTC's approved Master Plan, then the 
question is whether any decisions implementing segments outside your district, or not proximate to 
your home, would nevertheless affect or impact the planned trail near your residential property. 
Absent facts to the contrary, you have a foreseeable material financial interest in decisions 
regarding implementation of a planned trail. 
B. 
Public Generally Exception. 
The Act prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the decisions effect on the 
official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 


File No. A-18-086 
Page No. 5 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest 
-is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his 
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment. 
A "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 percent of: ( 1) the businesses or 
nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or residential real property; or 
(3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation l 8703(b ). ) 
You state that "RTC estimates that approximately 50% of the Santa Cruz County population 
currently lives within 1 mile of the rail line." Estimates of residences located closer to the Rail Line 
than I mile were not provided. Considering the proximity of your residence to a Rail Line right of 
way, we conclude that the effect on your real property appears unique compared to other residential 
properties in Santa Cruz County. Absent facts to the contrary, the public generally exception would 
not apply. 
For the above reasons, you may not make, participate in making, or influence the Rail Line 
decisions. When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a member 
of a planning commission) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or 
she must: ( 1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic 
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the 
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion 
and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, then please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
/ 
/ ( 
By: 
eser Jr., 
1or Counsel, Legal ]Division 
JMF:jgl

approximately 250 feet from a Rail Line right of way?

material financial effect on your residential property.
